hook: You already know how to fit a regression line and interpret coefficients — but real-world data rarely cooperates with the clean assumptions that make those estimates trustworthy. Endogeneity, non-constant variance, repeated observations, and time-dependent noise all quietly corrupt standard results in ways that are easy to miss. How do practicing econometricians detect and fix these problems, and what tools separate credible empirical work from misleading analysis?
concept: Every topic in this unit is a strategy for preserving valid inference when a core OLS assumption breaks down in a specific, realistic way — collectively captured by the goal of *identification and consistent estimation under violations of the classical linear model*.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Instrumental Variables and Two-Stage Least Squares (H)
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to recover causal estimates when your regressor is endogenous by using an instrument $Z$ that affects $X$ but influences $Y$ only through $X$, implemented via the two-stage least squares procedure.

Master how to detect non-constant error variance using diagnostic tests and correct your inference by computing heteroskedasticity-robust (sandwich) standard errors so that hypothesis tests remain valid.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Panel Data: Fixed and Random Effects (H)
topic_roadmap.description: Apply fixed-effects and random-effects estimators to longitudinal data so you can control for unobserved individual-level heterogeneity that would otherwise confound your estimates.

topic_roadmap.topic_name: Time Series: Stationarity, Autocorrelation, and ARIMA (H)
topic_roadmap.description: You will learn to diagnose and model temporal dependence in a time series — testing for stationarity, identifying autocorrelation structure, and fitting ARIMA models to produce reliable forecasts and valid inference.
